hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Presenting Your Company at a Trade Fair
lesson.menu_desc: Pitch your company and describe it with relative clauses -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Presenting at the Fair
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 13 -- Presenting at the Fair

listenings.text: Hi, thanks for stopping by. Let me give you the short version. We are Escape Turismo, an agency that specializes in luxury group travel across South America. Our flagship destination, which is Patagonia, sells out every single season. What makes us stand out is service. We are a well-established company that has handled corporate groups for over fifteen years, and every prospect who books with us gets a dedicated manager, someone who is available around the clock. If you like what you hear, take a brochure, and I will send you the full portfolio, which covers forty destinations. It would be great to work together.

Welcome to this short segment on presenting your company. When you describe what you do, relative clauses are your best friend. They let you add detail without starting a new sentence. Use who for people: the team who runs our groups. Use which or that for things: a platform that compares prices. Use where for places: the region where we specialize. Now, here is the key point for a polished pitch. There are two types. A defining clause gives essential information, and it takes no commas: the agency that books your trip. A non-defining clause just adds extra detail, and it takes commas, like this: our flagship, which is Patagonia, sells out fast. And remember, in that second type, never use the word that. Master this, and your pitch will sound clear, confident, and professional.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to present something proudly to an audience
a short, persuasive summary of your business
to be clearly better or different from others
the most important, best product or brand
a history of past results and achievements
a feature that makes people want to choose you
to focus on one particular area
existing for a long time and well respected
a printed booklet with information about a company
the total amount of business a company does in a period

paras: A trade fair floor is loud and crowded. Hundreds of companies compete for the same prospects, and every visitor who walks past your stand is deciding, in seconds, whether to stop. This is why the elevator pitch matters. It is the short, confident answer to the one question everyone asks: 'So, what does your company do?'

A great pitch is not a list of everything you offer. It is a story that shows what makes you stand out. The best presenters name one clear selling point -- the thing that only they do well -- and back it up with a track record. 'We are the agency that specializes in luxury group travel in South America,' one director says, 'and Patagonia, which is our flagship destination, sells out every season.' In two sentences, the prospect knows exactly who she is and why she is worth remembering.

The stand itself does the rest. A well-established company showcases its portfolio with a clean brochure, a cutting-edge screen, and a team who can answer any question. But the technology is never the point. The point is the human moment: a warm hello, a sharp pitch, and a follow-up that turns a curious visitor into a real client. Fairs are won not by the biggest stands, but by the companies that make every prospect feel seen.
reading.source: Adapted from a business communication guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The company with the biggest and most expensive stand always wins at a fair.
A sharp elevator pitch and one clear selling point make a company stand out and win prospects.
A good pitch is a complete list of every service the company offers.

items: The text says a trade fair floor is quiet and empty.
It says the floor is loud and crowded, with hundreds of companies competing.
A great pitch names one clear selling point rather than listing everything.
The second paragraph says the best presenters name one clear selling point, not a list of everything.
The text says the technology on the stand is the most important thing.
It says the technology is never the point -- the human moment is.
Fairs are won by the companies that make every prospect feel seen.
The last sentence says fairs are won by the companies that make every prospect feel seen.

gapfill.text: Relative clauses: use who for people, which/that for things, where for places. A defining clause has no commas (essential info); a non-defining clause has commas and never uses 'that' -- use which or who instead.

bank.label: Useful language for pitching and describing your company
items: We are an agency that specializes in luxury travel.
Our flagship destination, which is Patagonia, sells out fast.
What makes us stand out is our service.
Every prospect who visits our stand gets a brochure.
This is the platform where clients compare packages.
We have a strong track record with corporate groups.
Our main selling point is flexibility.
Let me showcase our portfolio for you.

lf.title: Complete the relative clause
items: visit our stand get a brochure.
is our flagship, sells out fast.

lf.text: Use who for people, which/that for things, where for places. Defining clauses (no commas) identify; non-defining clauses (commas, never 'that') just add extra detail.

items.situation: A prospect stops and asks, 'What does your company do?'
tips: 'We are an agency that specializes in...'

items.situation: You want to describe your flagship destination with extra detail.
tips: 'Patagonia, which is our flagship, sells out.'

items.situation: A prospect asks what makes you different from the stand next door.
tips: Back it with a track record.

items.situation: You want to describe your booking platform to a tech-curious visitor.
A prospect is interested and you want to follow up after the fair.
tips: 'I will send you the full portfolio, which covers...'

items: A prospect stops and asks what you do. Give a one-sentence pitch: 'We are an agency that...'.
Describe your flagship destination using a non-defining clause: 'Patagonia, which...'.
A prospect asks what makes you different. Name one selling point and back it with your track record.
Invite the prospect to follow up: showcase the brochure and offer the full portfolio.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = who, 2 = that, 3 = which, 4 = where, 5 = brochure.
Defining clause (no commas): 'the agency that books your trip'.
Non-defining clause (commas, never 'that'): 'our flagship, which is Patagonia, sells out'.
who = people, which/that = things, where = places.
answerkey.note: Relative clauses: who for people, which/that for things, where for places; defining = no commas, non-defining = commas and never 'that'.
